title: Paradise Regained - The Second Book
content:
آ آ آ آ Mean while the new-baptizï؟½d, who yet remainï؟½d
آ آ آ آ At Jordan with the Baptist, and had seen
آ آ آ آ Him whom they heard so late expresly callï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Jesus Messiah Son of God declarï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And on that high Authority had believï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And with him talkt, and with him lodgï؟½d, I mean
آ آ آ آ Andrew and Simon, famous after known
آ آ آ آ With others though in Holy Writ not namï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Now missing him thir joy so lately found,
آ آ آ آ So lately found, and so abruptly gone,
آ آ آ آ Began to doubt, and doubted many days,
آ آ آ آ And as the days increasï؟½d, increasï؟½d thir doubt:
آ آ آ آ Sometimes they thought he might be only shewn,
آ آ آ آ And for a time caught up to God, as once
آ آ آ آ Moses was in the Mount, and missing long;
آ آ آ آ And the great Thisbite who on fiery wheels
آ آ آ آ Rode up to Heaven, yet once again to come.
آ آ آ آ Therefore as those young Prophets then with care
آ آ آ آ Sought lost Eliah, so in each place these
آ آ آ آ Nigh to Bethabara; in Jerico
آ آ آ آ The City of Palms, ï؟½non, and Salem Old,
آ آ آ آ Machï؟½rus and each Town or City wallï؟½d
آ آ آ آ On this side the broad lake Genezaret,
آ آ آ آ Or in Perea, but returnï؟½d in vain.
آ آ آ آ Then on the bank of Jordan, by a Creek:
آ آ آ آ Where winds with Reeds, and Osiers whispï؟½ring play
آ آ آ آ Plain Fishermen, no greater men them call,
آ آ آ آ Close in a Cottage low together got
آ آ آ آ Thir unexpected loss and plaints out breathï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Alas, from what high hope to what relapse
آ آ آ آ Unlookï؟½d for are we fallï؟½n, our eyes beheld
آ آ آ آ Messiah certainly now come, so long
آ آ آ آ Expected of our Fathers; we have heard
آ آ آ آ His words, his wisdom full of grace and truth,
آ آ آ آ Now, now, for sure, deliverance is at hand,
آ آ آ آ The Kingdom shall to Israel be restorï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ Thus we rejoycï؟½d, but soon our joy is turnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Into perplexity and new amaze:
آ آ آ آ For whither is he gone, what accident
آ آ آ آ Hath rapt him from us? will he now retire
آ آ آ آ After appearance, and again prolong
آ آ آ آ Our expectation? God of Israel,
آ آ آ آ Send thy Messiah forth, the time is come;
آ آ آ آ Behold the Kings of the Earth how they oppress
آ آ آ آ Thy chosen, to what highth thir powï؟½r unjust
آ آ آ آ They have exalted, and behind them cast
آ آ آ آ All fear of thee, arise and vindicate
آ آ آ آ Thy Glory, free thy people from thir yoke,
آ آ آ آ But let us wait; thus far he hath performï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Sent his Anointed, and to us revealï؟½d him,
آ آ آ آ By his great Prophet, pointed at and shown,
آ آ آ آ In publick, and with him we have conversï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Let us be glad of this, and all our fears
آ آ آ آ Lay on his Providence; he will not fail
آ آ آ آ Nor will withdraw him now, nor will recall,
آ آ آ آ Mock us with his blest sight, then snatch him hence,
آ آ آ آ Soon we shall see our hope, our joy return.
آ آ آ آ Thus they out of their plaints new hope resume
آ آ آ آ To find whom at the first they found unsought:
آ آ آ آ But to his Mother Mary, when she saw
آ آ آ آ Others returnï؟½d from Baptism, not her Son,
آ آ آ آ Nor left at Jordan, tydings of him none;
آ آ آ آ Within her brest, though calm; her brest though pure,
آ آ آ آ Motherly cares and fears got head, and raisï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Some troublï؟½d thoughts, which she in sighs thus clad.
آ آ آ آ O what avails me now that honour high
آ آ آ آ To have conceivï؟½d of God, or that salute
آ آ آ آ Hale highly favourï؟½d, among women blest;
آ آ آ آ While I to sorrows am no less advancï؟½t,
آ آ آ آ And fears as eminent, above the lot
آ آ آ آ Of other women, by the birth I bore,
آ آ آ آ In such a season born when scarce a Shed
آ آ آ آ Could be obtainï؟½d to shelter him or me
آ آ آ آ From the bleak air; a Stable was our warmth,
آ آ آ آ A Manger his, yet soon enforcï؟½t to flye
آ آ آ آ Thence into Egypt, till the Murdï؟½rous King
آ آ آ آ Were dead, who sought his life, and missing fillï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With Infant blood the streets of Bethlehem;
آ آ آ آ From Egypt home returnï؟½d, in Nazareth
آ آ آ آ Hath been our dwelling many years, his life
آ آ آ آ Private, unactive, calm, contemplative,
آ آ آ آ Little suspicious to any King; but now
آ آ آ آ Full grown to Man, acknowledgï؟½d, as I hear,
آ آ آ آ By John the Baptist, and in publick shown,
آ آ آ آ Son ownï؟½d from Heaven by his Fatherï؟½s voice;
آ آ آ آ I lookï؟½t for some great change; to Honour? no,
آ آ آ آ But trouble, as old Simeon plain fore-told,
آ آ آ آ That to the fall and rising he should be
آ آ آ آ Of many in Israel, and to a sign
آ آ آ آ Spoken against, that through my very Soul
آ آ آ آ A sword shall pierce, this is my favourï؟½d lot,
آ آ آ آ My Exaltation to Afflictions high;
آ آ آ آ Afflicted I may be, it seems, and blest;
آ آ آ آ I will not argue that, nor will repine.
آ آ آ آ But where delays he now? some great intent
آ آ آ آ Conceals him: when twelve years he scarce had seen,
آ آ آ آ I lost him, but so found, as well I saw
آ آ آ آ He could not lose himself; but went about
آ آ آ آ His Fatherï؟½s business; what he meant I musï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Since understand; much more his absence now
آ آ آ آ Thus long to some great purpose he obscures.
آ آ آ آ But I to wait with patience am inurï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ My heart hath been a store-house long of things
آ آ آ آ And sayings laid up, portending strange events.
آ آ آ آ Thus Mary pondering oft, and oft to mind
آ آ آ آ Recalling what remarkably had passï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Since first her Salutation heard, with thoughts
آ آ آ آ Meekly composï؟½d awaited the fulfilling:
آ آ آ آ The while her Son tracing the Desert wild,
آ آ آ آ Sole but with holiest Meditations fed,
آ آ آ آ Into himself descended, and at once
آ آ آ آ All his great work to come before him set;
آ آ آ آ How to begin, how to accomplish best
آ آ آ آ His end of being on Earth, and mission high:
آ آ آ آ For Satan with slye preface to return
آ آ آ آ Had left him vacant, and with speed was gon
آ آ آ آ Up to the middle Region of thick Air,
آ آ آ آ Where all his Potentates in Council sate;
آ آ آ آ There without sign of boast, or sign of joy,
آ آ آ آ Sollicitous and blank he thus began.
آ آ آ آ Princes, Heavens antient Sons, ï؟½thereal Thrones,
آ آ آ آ Demonian Spirits now, from the Element
آ آ آ آ Each of his reign allotted, rightlier callï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Powers of Fire, Air, Water, and Earth beneath,
آ آ آ آ So may we hold our place and these mild seats
آ آ آ آ Without new trouble; such an Enemy
آ آ آ آ Is risï؟½n to invade us, who no less
آ آ آ آ Threatï؟½ns then our expulsion down to Hell;
آ آ آ آ I, as I undertook, and with the vote
آ آ آ آ Consenting in full frequence was impowrï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Have found him, viewï؟½d him, tasted him, but find
آ آ آ آ Far other labour to be undergon
آ آ آ آ Then when I dealt with Adam first of Men,
آ آ آ آ Though Adam by his Wives allurement fell,
آ آ آ آ However to this Man inferior far,
آ آ آ آ If he be Man by Mothers side at least,
آ آ آ آ With more then humane gifts from Heaven adornï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Perfections absolute, Graces divine,
آ آ آ آ And amplitude of mind to greatest Deeds.
آ آ آ آ Therefore I am returnï؟½d, lest confidence
آ آ آ آ Of my success with Eve in Paradise
آ آ آ آ Deceive ye to perswasion over-sure
آ آ آ آ Of like succeeding here; I summon all
آ آ آ آ Rather to be in readiness, with hand
آ آ آ آ Or counsel to assist; lest I who erst
آ آ آ آ Thought none my equal, now be over-matchï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ So spake the old Serpent doubting, and from all
آ آ آ آ With clamour was assurï؟½d thir utmost aid
آ آ آ آ At his command; when from amidst them rose
آ آ آ آ Belial the dissolutest Spirit that fell,
آ آ آ آ The sensuallest, and after Asmodai
آ آ آ آ The fleshliest Incubus, and thus advisï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Set women in his eye and in his walk,
آ آ آ آ Among daughters of men the fairest found;
آ آ آ آ Many are in each Region passing fair
آ آ آ آ As the noon Skie; more like to Goddesses
آ آ آ آ Then Mortal Creatures, graceful and discreet,
آ آ آ آ Expert in amorous Arts, enchanting tongues
آ آ آ آ Perswasive, Virgin majesty with mild
آ آ آ آ And sweet allayï؟½d, yet terrible to approach,
آ آ آ آ Skillï؟½d to retire, and in retiring draw
آ آ آ آ Hearts after them tanglï؟½d in Amorous Nets.
آ آ آ آ Such object hath the power to softï؟½n and tame
آ آ آ آ Severest temper, smooth the ruggedï؟½st brow,
آ آ آ آ Enerve, and with voluptuous hope dissolve,
آ آ آ آ Draw out with credulous desire, and lead
آ آ آ آ At will the manliest, resolutest brest,
آ آ آ آ As the Magnetic hardest Iron draws.
آ آ آ آ Women, when nothing else, beguilï؟½d the heart
آ آ آ آ Of wisest Solomon, and made him build,
آ آ آ آ And made him bow to the Gods of his Wives.
آ آ آ آ To whom quick answer Satan thus returnï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Belial, in much uneven scale thou weighï؟½st
آ آ آ آ All others by thy self; because of old
آ آ آ آ Thou thy self doatï؟½st on womankind, admiring
آ آ آ آ Thir shape, thir colour, and attractive grace,
آ آ آ آ None are, thou thinkï؟½st, but taken with such toys.
آ آ آ آ Before the Flood thou with thy lusty Crew,
آ آ آ آ False titlï؟½d Sons of God, roaming the Earth
آ آ آ آ Cast wanton eyes on the daughters of men,
آ آ آ آ And couplï؟½d with them, and begot a race.
آ آ آ آ Have we not seen, or by relation heard,
آ آ آ آ In Courts and Regal Chambers how thou lurkï؟½st,
آ آ آ آ In Wood or Grove by mossie Fountain side,
آ آ آ آ In Valley or Green Meadow to way-lay
آ آ آ آ Some beauty rare, Calisto, Clymene,
آ آ آ آ Daphne, or Semele, Antiopa,
آ آ آ آ Or Amymone, Syrinx, many more
آ آ آ آ Too long, then layï؟½st thy scapes on names adorï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Apollo, Neptune, Jupiter, or Pan,
آ آ آ آ Satyr, or Fawn, or Silvan? But these haunts
آ آ آ آ Delight not all; among the Sons of Men,
آ آ آ آ How many have with a smile made small account
آ آ آ آ Of beauty and her lures, easily scornï؟½d
آ آ آ آ All her assaults, on worthier things intent?
آ آ آ آ Remember that Pellean Conquerour,
آ آ آ آ A youth, how all the Beauties of the East
آ آ آ آ He slightly viewï؟½d, and slightly over-passï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ How hee sirnamï؟½d of Africa dismissï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In his prime youth the fair Iberian maid.
آ آ آ آ For Solomon he livï؟½d at ease, and full
آ آ آ آ Of honour, wealth, high fare, aimï؟½d not beyond
آ آ آ آ Higher design then to enjoy his State;
آ آ آ آ Thence to the bait of Women lay exposï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ But he whom we attempt is wiser far
آ آ آ آ Then Solomon, of more exalted mind,
آ آ آ آ Made and set wholly on the accomplishment
آ آ آ آ Of greatest things; what woman will you find,
آ آ آ آ Though of this Age the wonder and the fame,
آ آ آ آ On whom his leisure will vouchsafe an eye
آ آ آ آ Of fond desire? or should she confident,
آ آ آ آ As sitting Queen adorï؟½d on Beauties Throne,
آ آ آ آ Descend with all her winning charms begirt
آ آ آ آ To enamour, as the Zone of Venus once
آ آ آ آ Wrought that effect on Jove, so Fables tell;
آ آ آ آ How would one look from his Majestick brow
آ آ آ آ Seated as on the top of Vertues hill,
آ آ آ آ Discountï؟½nance her despisï؟½d, and put to rout
آ آ آ آ All her array; her female pride deject,
آ آ آ آ Or turn to reverent awe? for Beauty stands
آ آ آ آ In the admiration only of weak minds
آ آ آ آ Led captive; cease to admire, and all her Plumes
آ آ آ آ Fall flat and shrink into a trivial toy,
آ آ آ آ At every sudden slighting quite abasht:
آ آ آ آ Therefore with manlier objects we must try
آ آ آ آ His constancy, with such as have more shew
آ آ آ آ Of worth, of honour, glory, and popular praise;
آ آ آ آ Rocks whereon greatest men have oftest wreckï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Or that which only seems to satisfie
آ آ آ آ Lawful desires of Nature, not beyond;
آ آ آ آ And now I know he hungers where no food
آ آ آ آ Is to be found, in the wide Wilderness;
آ آ آ آ The rest commit to me, I shall let pass
آ آ آ آ No advantage, and his strength as oft assay.
آ آ آ آ He ceasï؟½d, and heard thir grant in loud acclaim;
آ آ آ آ Then forthwith to him takes a chosen band
آ آ آ آ Of Spirits likest to himself in guile
آ آ آ آ To be at hand, and at his beck appear,
آ آ آ آ If cause were to unfold some active Scene
آ آ آ آ Of various persons each to know his part;
آ آ آ آ Then to the Desert takes with these his flight;
آ آ آ آ Where still from shade to shade the Son of God
آ آ آ آ After forty days fasting had remainï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Now hungring first, and to himself thus said.
آ آ آ آ Where will this end? four times ten days I have passï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Wandring this woody maze, and humane food
آ آ آ آ Nor tasted, nor had appetite; that Fast
آ آ آ آ To Vertue I impute not, or count part
آ آ آ آ Of what I suffer here; if Nature need not,
آ آ آ آ Or God support Nature without repast
آ آ آ آ Though needing, what praise is it to endure?
آ آ آ آ But now I feel I hunger, which declares,
آ آ آ آ Nature hath need of what she asks; yet God
آ آ آ آ Can satisfie that need some other way,
آ آ آ آ Though hunger still remain: so it remain
آ آ آ آ Without this bodies wasting, I content me,
آ آ آ آ And from the sting of Famine fear no harm,
آ آ آ آ Nor mind it, fed with better thoughts that feed
آ آ آ آ Mee hungring more to do my Fathers will.
آ آ آ آ It was the hour of night, when thus the Son
آ آ آ آ Communï؟½d in silent walk, then laid him down
آ آ آ آ Under the hospitable covert nigh
آ آ آ آ Of Trees thick interwoven; there he slept,
آ آ آ آ And dreamï؟½d, as appetite is wont to dream,
آ آ آ آ Of meats and drinks, Natures refreshment sweet;
آ آ آ آ Him thought, he by the Brook of Cherith stood
آ آ آ آ And saw the Ravens with their horny beaks
آ آ آ آ Food to Elijah bringing Even and Morn,
آ آ آ آ Though ravenous, taught to abstain from what they brought:
آ آ آ آ He saw the Prophet also how he fled
آ آ آ آ Into the Desert, and how there he slept
آ آ آ آ Under a Juniper; then how awakt,
آ آ آ آ He found his Supper on the coals preparï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And by the Angel was bid rise and eat,
آ آ آ آ And eat the second time after repose,
آ آ آ آ The strength whereof sufficï؟½d him forty days;
آ آ آ آ Sometimes that with Elijah he partook,
آ آ آ آ Or as a guest with Daniel at his pulse.
آ آ آ آ Thus wore out night, and now the Herald Lark
آ آ آ آ Left his ground-nest, high towring to descry
آ آ آ آ The morns approach, and greet her with his Song:
آ آ آ آ As lightly from his grassy Couch up rose
آ آ آ آ Our Saviour, and found all was but a dream,
آ آ آ آ Fasting he went to sleep, and fasting wakï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Up to a hill anon his steps he rearï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ From whose high top to ken the prospect round,
آ آ آ آ If Cottage were in view, Sheep-cote or Herd;
آ آ آ آ But Cottage, Herd or Sheep-cote none he saw,
آ آ آ آ Only in a bottom saw a pleasant Grove,
آ آ آ آ With chaunt of tuneful Birds resounding loud;
آ آ آ آ Thither he bent his way, determinï؟½d there
آ آ آ آ To rest at noon, and entrï؟½d soon the shade
آ آ آ آ High rooft and walks beneath, and alleys brown
آ آ آ آ That openï؟½d in the midst a woody Scene,
آ آ آ آ Natures own work it seemï؟½d (Nature taught Art)
آ آ آ آ And to a Superstitious eye the haunt
آ آ آ آ Of Wood-Gods and Wood-Nymphs; he viewï؟½d it round,
آ آ آ آ When suddenly a man before him stood,
آ آ آ آ Not rustic as before, but seemlier clad,
آ آ آ آ As one in City, or Court, or Palace bred,
آ آ آ آ And with fair speech these words to him addressï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ With granted leave officious I return,
آ آ آ آ But much more wonder that the Son of God
آ آ آ آ In this wild solitude so long should bide
آ آ آ آ Of all things destitute, and well I know,
آ آ آ آ Not without hunger. Others of some note,
آ آ آ آ As story tells, have trod this Wilderness;
آ آ آ آ The Fugitive Bond-woman with her Son
آ آ آ آ Out cast Nebaioth, yet found he relief
آ آ آ آ By a providing Angel; all the race
آ آ آ آ Of Israel here had famishï؟½d, had not God
آ آ آ آ Rainï؟½d from Heaven Manna, and that Prophet bold
آ آ آ آ Native of Thebes wandring here was fed
آ آ آ آ Twice by a voice inviting him to eat.
آ آ آ آ Of thee these forty days none hath regard,
آ آ آ آ Forty and more deserted here indeed.
آ آ آ آ To whom thus Jesus; what concludï؟½st thou hence?
آ آ آ آ They all had need, I as thou seest have none.
آ آ آ آ How hast thou hunger then? Satan replyï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Tell me if Food were now before thee set,
آ آ آ آ Wouldï؟½st thou not eat? Thereafter as I like
آ آ آ آ The giver, answerï؟½d Jesus. Why should that
آ آ آ آ Cause thy refusal, said the subtle Fiend,
آ آ آ آ Hast thou not right to all Created things,
آ آ آ آ Owe not all Creatures by just right to thee
آ آ آ آ Duty and Service, nor to stay till bid,
آ آ آ آ But tender all their power? nor mention I

آ آ آ آ Nature ashamï؟½d, or better to express,

آ آ آ آ From all the Elements her choicest store
آ آ آ آ To treat thee as beseems, and as her Lord
آ آ آ آ With honour, only deign to sit and eat.
آ آ آ آ He spake no dream, for as his words had end,
آ آ آ آ Our Saviour lifting up his eyes beheld
آ آ آ آ In ample space under the broadest shade
آ آ آ آ A Table richly spred, in regal mode,
آ آ آ آ With dishes pillï؟½d, and meats of noblest sort
آ آ آ آ And savour, Beasts of chase, or Fowl of game,
آ آ آ آ In pastry built, or from the spit, or boylï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Gris-amber-steamï؟½d; all Fish from Sea or Shore,
آ آ آ آ Freshet, or purling Brook, of shell or fin,
آ آ آ آ And exquisitest name, for which was drainï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Pontus and Lucrine Bay, and Afric Coast.
آ آ آ آ Alas how simple, to these Cates comparï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Was that crude Apple that diverted Eve!
آ آ آ آ And at a stately side-board by the wine
آ آ آ آ That fragrant smell diffusï؟½d, in order stood
آ آ آ آ Tall stripling youths rich clad, of fairer hew
آ آ آ آ Then Ganymed or Hylas, distant more
آ آ آ آ Under the Trees now tripï؟½d, now solemn stood
آ آ آ آ Nymphs of Dianaï؟½s train, and Naiades
آ آ آ آ With fruits and flowers from Amaltheaï؟½s horn,
آ آ آ آ And Ladies of thï؟½ Hesperides, that seemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Fairer then feignï؟½d of old, or fablï؟½d since
آ آ آ آ Of Fairy Damsels met in Forest wide
آ آ آ آ By Knights of Logres, or of Lyones,
آ آ آ آ Lancelot or Pelleas, or Pellenore,
آ آ آ آ And all the while Harmonious Airs were heard
آ آ آ آ Of chiming strings, or charming pipes and winds
آ آ آ آ Of gentlest gale Arabian odors fannï؟½d
آ آ آ آ From their soft wings, and Floraï؟½s earliest smells.
آ آ آ آ Such was the Splendour, and the Tempter now
آ آ آ آ His invitation earnestly renewï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ What doubts the Son of God to sit and eat?
آ آ آ آ These are not Fruits forbidden, no interdict
آ آ آ آ Defends the touching of these viands pure,
آ آ آ آ Thir taste no knowledge works, at least of evil,
آ آ آ آ But life preserves, destroys lifeï؟½s enemy,
آ آ آ آ Hunger, with sweet restorative delight.
آ آ آ آ All these are Spirits of Air, and Woods, and Springs,
آ آ آ آ Thy gentle Ministers, who come to pay
آ آ آ آ Thee homage, and acknowledge thee thir Lord:
آ آ آ آ What doubtï؟½st thou Son of God? sit down and eat.
آ آ آ آ To whom thus Jesus temperately replyï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ Saidï؟½st thou not that to all things I had right?
آ آ آ آ And who withholds my powï؟½r that right to use?
آ آ آ آ Shall I receive by gift what of my own,
آ آ آ آ When and where likes me best, I can command?
آ آ آ آ I can at will, doubt not, as soon as thou,
آ آ آ آ Command a Table in this Wilderness,
آ آ آ آ And call swift flights of Angels ministrant
آ آ آ آ Arrayï؟½d in Glory on my cup to attend:
آ آ آ آ Why shouldst thou then obtrude this diligence,
آ آ آ آ In vain, where no acceptance it can find,
آ آ آ آ And with my hunger what has thou to do?
آ آ آ آ Thy pompous Delicacies I contemn,
آ آ آ آ And count thy specious gifts no gifts but guiles.
آ آ آ آ To whom thus answerï؟½d Satan malecontent:
آ آ آ آ That I have also power to give thou seest,
آ آ آ آ If of that powï؟½r I bring thee voluntary
آ آ آ آ What I might have bestowï؟½d on whom I pleasï؟½d,

آ آ آ آ What I can do or offer is suspect;

آ آ آ آ By hunger, that each other Creature tames,

آ آ آ آ Thy temperance invincible besides,
آ آ آ آ For no allurement yields to appetite,
آ آ آ آ And all thy heart is set on high designs,

آ آ آ آ Great acts require great means of enterprise,
آ آ آ آ Thou art unknown, unfriended, low of birth,
آ آ آ آ A Carpenter thy Father known, thy self
آ آ آ آ Bred up in poverty and streights at home;
آ آ آ آ Lost in a Desert here and hunger-bit:
آ آ آ آ Which way or from what hope dost thou aspire
آ آ آ آ To greatness? whence Authority derivï؟½st,

آ آ آ آ Or at thy heels the dizzy Multitude,
آ آ آ آ Longer then thou canst feed them on thy cost?
آ آ آ آ Money brings Honour, Friends, Conquest, and Realms;
آ آ آ آ What raisï؟½d Antipater the Edomite,
آ آ آ آ And his Son Herod placï؟½d on Judaï؟½s Throne;

آ آ آ آ Therefore, if at great things thou wouldst arrive,
آ آ آ آ Get Riches first, get Wealth, and Treasure heap,
آ آ آ آ Not difficult, if thou hearken to me,
آ آ آ آ Riches are mine, Fortune is in my hand;
آ آ آ آ They whom I favour thrive in wealth amain,

آ آ آ آ To whom thus Jesus patiently replyï؟½d;

آ آ آ آ Among the Heathen, (for throughout the World

آ آ آ آ Riches are needless then, both for themselves,
آ آ آ آ And for thy reason why they should be sought,

meaning:
This evocative piece by John Milton represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of John Milton's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
